items.headline: Spanien feiert seine Fußballweltmeister
items.summary: Die spanische Fußballnationalmannschaft ist nach ihrem WM-Sieg euphorisch in der Heimat empfangen worden - von Fans, König Felipe und dem Ministerpräsidenten Sanchez. Von Nicholas Buschschlüter, zzt. ARD Madrid.

items.headline: Schachgroßmeisterin Polgar will nicht Ungarns Präsidentin werden
items.summary: In Ungarn gab es einen Dämpfer für den neuen Premier Magyar: Seine Wunschkandidatin für die Präsidentschaft, die Schachgroßmeisterin Judit Polgar, hat abgesagt. Die "historische Verantwortung" sei zu groß. Von Oliver Soos.

items.headline: Bahn-Konkurrent Italo bestellt 26 Schnellzüge bei Siemens
items.summary: Erst vor wenigen Tagen ebnete die Bundesnetzagentur den Einstieg des italienischen Zugunternehmens Italo in das deutsche Fernverkehrsnetz. Jetzt konkretisiert Italo seine Pläne - mit einer Bestellung für neue Schnellzüge.

items.headline: Gedenken an Hitler-Attentat im Bendlerblock - mit Zwischenfall
items.summary: Die Bundesregierung hat in Berlin an den Widerstand gegen die nationalsozialistische Gewaltherrschaft erinnert. Anlass ist der heutige Jahrestag des Attentats auf Hitler. Ein Kranz der AfD sorgte vor dem Gedenken für ein Handgemenge.

items.headline: USA: Mexikanischer Drogenboss "El Mayo" zu lebenslanger Haft verurteilt
items.summary: In den USA ist der frühere mexikanische Drogenboss "El Mayo" zu lebenslanger Haft verurteilt worden. Der 76-jährige Mitbegründer des berüchtigten Sinaloa-Kartells bat in der Verhandlung um Verzeihung.

items.headline: Machtkampf: AfD-Bundesvorstand setzt Untersuchungskommission ein
items.summary: Der AfD-Bundesvorstand verzichtet im Machtkampf mit der Parteispitze in Nordrhein-Westfalen auf sofortige Konsequenzen. Er setzt aber eine Untersuchungskommission ein, die den Landesverband durchleuchten soll.

items.headline: Neuer Premier Burnham will Briten wieder Hoffnung geben
items.summary: Andy Burnham, der siebte britische Premier in zehn Jahren, muss ein tief gespaltenes Land einen. Seine Pläne sind ehrgeizig: Er will die Lebenshaltungskosten senken, die Wirtschaft ankurbeln und Sozialsysteme entlasten. Von Lisa-Maria Röhling.

items.headline: Marktbericht: DAX-Hängepartie geht weiter
items.summary: Die Eskalation der Lage im Nahen Osten nagt ebenso an den Nerven der Anleger wie die Sorge um eine Korrektur im KI-Sektor. DAX-Anleger hielten sich daher zum Wochenstart bedeckt.

items.headline: AliExpress: EU-Kommission verhängt DSA-Rekordstrafe
items.summary: Der chinesische Onlinemarktplatz AliExpress hat über Jahre gegen den Digital Services Act verstoßen und muss nun 550 Millionen Euro zahlen.

items.headline: Microsoft arbeitet an Lösung für WSUS-Störungen
items.summary: Die Update-Software WSUS hat seit vergangener Woche mit Problemen zu kämpfen, hat Microsoft nun eingeräumt. Es gibt erste Fixes.

items.headline: WTF: Billionenbeträge berechnet: Abrechnungsfehler bei Amazon Web Services
items.summary: Kunden wurden plötzlich astronomische Kosten für Amazon Web Services angezeigt. Dahinter steckt ein technischer Fehler.

items.headline: Moonshot AI präsentiert Kimi K3: Chinas Antwort auf OpenAI und Anthropic
items.summary: Chinas KI-Start-up Moonshot AI setzt die USA mit seinem neuen Sprachmodell unter Druck. Wie reagieren Washington und das Silicon Valley auf den Wettbewerb?

items.headline: LHS 1140 b: Erstmals Atmosphäre bei erdähnlichem Exoplaneten nachgewiesen
items.summary: Seit seiner Entdeckung gilt LHS 1140 b als besonders interessant für die Suche nach außerirdischem Leben. Nun wurde bei dem Exoplaneten Helium entdeckt.

items.headline: Google Chrome: Außerplanmäßiges zweites Update in der Woche
items.summary: Google aktualisiert Chrome eigentlich jeden Mittwoch. Diese Woche folgt ein zweites Update, das mehrere kritische Lücken schließt.

items.headline: 7-Zip: Update stopft Codeschmuggel-Lücke
items.summary: Das Packprogramm 7-Zip patzt bei der Verarbeitung präparierter xz-Daten. Das kann zum Ausführen eingeschmuggelten Codes führen.

items.headline: EU-Vorgaben: Google muss Android für fremde KI-Assistenten komplett öffnen
items.summary: Die EU verpflichtet Google, Android für KI-Assistenten von Fremdanbietern zu öffnen. Ab Android 18 sollen sie die gleichen Zugriffsrechte wie Gemini erhalten.

items.headline: Anzeige: Inbus Schraubendreher mit Ratsche befristet im Amazon-Angebot
items.summary: Ein Ratschenschraubendreher von Inbus mit zwölf Bits ist kurze Zeit bei Amazon günstiger als im 90-Tage-Schnitt. (Technik/Hardware)

items.headline: Wingtech: Nexperia kann Kapazitäten wieder etwas steigern
items.summary: Die deutsche und europäische Automobilindustrie leidet weiterhin darunter, dass die USA den Nexperia-Eigner Wingtech auf die Blacklist gesetzt haben. Langsam erholt sich Nexperia. (Chipknappheit, Prozessor)

items.headline: (g+) House & Home: How smart tech is being weaponised in the home
items.summary: Remote access to domestic devices is increasingly being used to manipulate, intimidate and disturb. Policymakers and tech companies are grappling with safeguards. Von Emma Jacobs (Security, Tracking)

items.headline: Marvel Cinematic Universe: Marvel zeigt ersten Trailer zu Avengers: Doomsday
items.summary: Marvel zeigt den ersten Trailer zu Avengers: Doomsday. Doctor Doom führt die Bedrohung an, Steve Rogers kehrt zurück. (Marvel, Film)

items.headline: FTTH: Telekom bereitet Einsatz von 50G-PON für Kunden vor
items.summary: Die Deutsche Telekom plant den Einsatz von 50G-PON als Nachfolger von XGS-PON. Erste Labor-Tests finden bereits statt, um die Netze für Multigigabit-Bandbreiten vorzubereiten. (Telekom, Huawei)

items.headline: Anzeige: Wetterstation mit Außensensor und WLAN reduziert bei Amazon
items.summary: Eine smarte Wetterstation von SwitchBot mit Innenthermometer, Außensensor und Matter-fähigem WLAN-Hub gibt es bei Amazon zum Aktionspreis. (Technik/Hardware, Matter)

items.headline: China-Support für Pentagon: Microsoft-Mittelsmänner vertrauten blind auf Codesicherheit
items.summary: Neue Informationen zeigen im Detail, wie Microsoft getrickst hat, um für den IT-Support im Pentagon chinesische Ingenieure einsetzen zu können. (Microsoft, Datenschutz)

items.headline: Fast so gut wie Fable 5: Alibaba veröffentlicht Qwen-3.8 und KI-Software-Stack
items.summary: Die Modellgewichte von Qwen-3.8 sollen veröffentlicht werden. Teile seines KI-Software-Stacks hat Alibaba schon öffentlich gemacht - nicht uneigennützig. (KI, Fable)

items.headline: Trump imposes 50% tariff on Canadian imports
items.summary: The duties take effect within 30 days and mark a major escalation in trade tensions between the North American neighbours.

items.headline: Yemen's Houthis announce 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia
items.summary: The Houthis say the embargo begins immediately and is a response to a Saudi blockade of ports and airports in Yemen.

items.headline: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for death of soldiers
items.summary: The US says the attacks were aimed at degrading Iran's capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

items.headline: Notorious drug cartel leader Ismael Zambada sentenced to life in US prison
items.summary: Known as "El Mayo", the co-founder the Sinaloa Cartel was also ordered to pay $15bn after he admitted to sending vast amounts of drugs to the US.

items.headline: Merger deal between Paramount and Warner Bros paused by judge
items.summary: US court temporarily stops Paramount and Warner Bros merging after 12 states launched legal action.

items.headline: Tate brothers appear in US court for extradition proceedings
items.summary: They were arrested on Saturday after the UK announced dozens of new charges, including rape and sex trafficking, which they deny.

items.headline: 'If only people knew what a tick could do' - the deadly meat allergies caused by tick bites
items.summary: Mammalian meat allergy is a growing problem along Australia's east coast - and it's caused by tick bites.

items.headline: ‘All I think about is her’: Families demand answers as Nigeria's boat disasters keep happening
items.summary: Hundreds of lives are lost each year in Nigeria as a result of boat accidents yet nothing seems to change.

items.headline: Thousands of websites taken down for illegal World Cup streams
items.summary: More than 1,000 illegal streaming sites were shut down in the US during the tournament, with a similar amount blocked in Colombia.

items.headline: Ofcom unable to take further legal action against suicide forum
items.summary: The regulator said it had "no further legal routes available" under the Online Safety Act.

items.headline: AliExpress fined record €550m by EU for allowing sale of illegal goods
items.summary: The Chinese retailer allowed unsafe toys and counterfeit clothes to be sold on its site, says the EU.

items.headline: Tech Now
items.summary: Ammie Sekhon goes behind the scenes of WWE to see the tech that supports the show.

items.headline: China's Moonshot AI claims Kimi K3 can rival OpenAI and Anthropic
items.summary: The company has unveiled a massive new artificial intelligence model it says can take on top American firms.

items.headline: Trump Media to sell instant access to 'market-moving' social posts
items.summary: Trump Media is launching a fast, paid feed to its most influential posts for Wall Street traders.

items.headline: TikTok faces Ofcom investigation over child age checks
items.summary: It follows a review by the regulator in May that criticised the platform for not being "safe enough" for children.

items.headline: Teen hackers who live streamed cyber-attack on TfL jailed
items.summary: Owen Flowers and Thalha Jubair were convicted for their roles in the attack, which led to large costs for Transport for London.

items.headline: The Space Force is now seeking to buy up to $30 billion in rocket launches
items.summary: The Trump administration is asking the Space Force to do a lot. This will require more launches.

items.headline: AT&T loses key ruling in bid to stop offering basic phone service in California
items.summary: AT&T suffers setback but will keep asking court and FCC to preempt state rules.

items.headline: RIP bargain bin: The price impact of Sony's disc-free PlayStation plan
items.summary: Used discs are often cheaper than even deep digital discounts.

items.headline: F1 in Belgium: Machine learning algorithms are ruining the sport
items.summary: Many will say Spa is the best racetrack on earth, but not for these F1 cars.

items.headline: AliExpress hit with record $625M fine after failing to make EU-ordered fixes
items.summary: Online retailer AliExpress says it's shocked by largest DSA fine yet.

items.headline: The first trailer for Avengers: Doomsday is finally here
items.summary: "Something's coming. Something we may not be able to deter."

items.headline: Pay up or not? Ransomware surge has victims facing tough choices.
items.summary: Governments look at banning ransom payments in face of increasingly sophisticated threats.

items.headline: Beyond grep: The case for a context-rich AI coding harness
items.summary: Augment Code's Vinay Perneti talks models, harnesses, and context.

items.headline: Google is working on a new AI chip designed to make Gemini more efficient
items.summary: Alphabet, Google's parent company, is reportedly working on a new chip designed to make its Gemini models run much more efficiently.

items.headline: AI’s most important protocol is getting a little bit easier to use
items.summary: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is one of the basic building blocks of AI interoperability, giving AI models a secure way to access external data sources and services. It’s the plumbing that lets a chatbot reach into your calendar, your database, or your internal tools, instead

items.headline: X relaunches a rebuilt Android app after year-long effort
items.summary: X says the rebuilt version of its Android app is now available globally.

items.headline: OpenAI is scared of open-weight models. Should the US be?
items.summary: Talk of banning Chinese-made open-weight LLMs reveals the challenge of turning AI into a business.

items.headline: Adobe camera app’s new feature will critique your photos using AI
items.summary: Adobe's Project Indigo can now remove all kinds of backgrounds from photos you snap using the app.

items.headline: YouTube clarifies policies around AI slop and upsetting videos
items.summary: YouTube has updated its monetization policies to more clearly define the kinds of AI-generated and low-quality videos that can’t earn ad revenue.

items.headline: What to watch for after Jensen Huang’s Japan visit
items.summary: Jensen Huang left Tokyo with deals spanning Japan's entire tech ecosystem.

items.headline: Can an Apple lawsuit derail OpenAI’s hardware plans?
items.summary: On the latest episode of Equity, we debate whether Apple's lawsuit will cast a shadow over OpenAi's much-discussed plans to get into hardware and go public.

items.headline: China delivers a one-two punch to America’s AI dominance
items.summary: China's leading AI companies are ramping up the pressure on Silicon Valley, as Moonshot and Alibaba unveiled models they claim can go toe-to-toe with the best from OpenAI and Anthropic at a fraction of the cost. The rapid-fire releases suggest America's lead at the AI frontier is

items.summary: Dave Eggers attends the "The Turning Point: To Be Destroyed" premiere. | Image: John Lamparski/Getty Images for Tribeca Festival Last year, Sam Altman invited author Dave Eggers to give a talk to around 200 OpenAI staffers. The man has written countless novels, screenplays, piece

items.headline: TikTok is testing an AI likeness detection tool
items.summary: TikTok is starting to test an opt-in tool that scans for AI likenesses and lets creators report them to the company, as spotted by social media consultant Matt Navarra. The tool is initially being tested with "some" US creators, TikTok US spokesperson Zachary Kizer tells The Verg

items.headline: Apple’s plot to crush OpenAI
items.summary: Apple is suing OpenAI. The complaint is readable and intense, as these things often are, though many experts seem to think many of the allegations are just the ways things are done. So what does Apple really want here, and why is it picking such a public fight with OpenAI? On thi

items.headline: The AI compute gap: Enterprises are buying infrastructure faster than they can measure what it costs
items.summary: Across 107 enterprises, AI infrastructure spending is accelerating well ahead of the ability to see or steer its economics. Most organizations run their AI on a familiar base of hyperscalers and model-provider APIs, yet the next dollar is aimed at specialized compute almost none

items.headline: The agent security gap: 54% of enterprises have already had an AI agent incident, and most still let agents share credentials
items.summary: Across 107 enterprises, AI agents are being given real access to systems and data while the controls meant to contain them lag behind. More than half have already had a confirmed agent security incident or a near-miss; only about a third give every agent its own scoped identity,

items.headline: The AI context gap: Enterprise AI organizations have a trust problem, not a retrieval problem — and most are still building the fix
items.summary: Across 101 enterprises, the infrastructure that feeds AI agents their business context is being built faster than it can be trusted. Retrieval-augmented generation is already the default context source, and provider-native retrieval has quietly overtaken the dedicated vector data

items.headline: The agent evaluation gap: Enterprise AI organizations have a reality-alignment problem, not a coverage problem — and most are shipping to production anyway
items.summary: Across 157 enterprises, organizations are granting AI agents more autonomy while trusting the evaluations meant to gate that autonomy less. Half have already shipped an agent that passed their internal evaluations and then failed a customer in production; only one in twenty fully

items.headline: Agentic orchestration: Enterprise AI organizations have a deployment problem, not a platform problem — and most are calling chatbots agents
items.summary: Across 101 enterprises, agent orchestration is consolidating onto model-provider platforms — Anthropic’s Claude leads by a wide margin — chosen for the gravity of the underlying model and judged on reliable multi-step execution. But the ambition runs well ahead of the reality: mo

items.headline: Google just redesigned the search box for the first time in 25 years — here’s why it matters more than you think.
items.summary: For a quarter century, the Google search box has been one of the most recognizable interfaces in computing: a thin white rectangle, a blinking cursor, a few typed words, and a list of blue links. On Tuesday, Google will formally retire that paradigm. At its annual I/O developer c

items.headline: Railway secures $100 million to challenge AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure
items.summary: Railway, a San Francisco-based cloud platform that has quietly amassed two million developers without spending a dollar on marketing, announced Thursday that it raised $100 million in a Series B funding round, as surging demand for artificial intelligence applications exposes the

items.headline: AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring
items.summary: The next time you apply for a job, AI may screen your résumé before any human sees it. But there’s good reason to question whether AI will judge you fairly. Researchers already know that LLMs pick up human biases from their training data. New research suggests that LLMs can also

items.headline: The risk of weather data sabotage is rising
items.summary: Every morning, airline dispatchers, grid operators, and farmers around the world make decisions based on the same thing: a weather forecast. While these forecasts are something that most people glance at for two seconds, weather predictions influence major strategic decisions in

items.headline: Meet GPT-Red: an LLM super-hacker OpenAI built to make its models safer
items.summary: OpenAI has built an LLM super-hacker called GPT-Red that it uses as a sparring partner to help its other models boost their defenses against cyberattacks. Last week the company released the latest version of its flagship LLM, GPT-5.6. OpenAI says that training it against GPT-Red

items.headline: Anthropic found a hidden space where Claude puzzles over concepts

items.headline: The foundational elements of AI architecture that IT leaders need to scale

items.summary: Now that Google has changed how its usage quotas are tallied, you might not get as many AI responses as you did before.

items.headline: Prompt Injection Attacks Are Thwarting AI Hacking Agents
items.summary: “Context bombing” tricks malicious AI agents into shutting down before they can do harm.

items.headline: San Francisco Demands Apple and Google Delete AI ‘Nudify’ Apps From App Stores
items.summary: The City Attorney’s Office sent the tech giants cease-and-desist letters this week telling them to stop profiting from 13 “face-swap” apps that are overwhelmingly used to target women and girls.

items.headline: A Humanoid Company Backed by Eric Trump Is Preparing Its Robots for War
items.summary: The CEO of Foundation Future Industries, which counts the president’s son as its chief strategy adviser, tells WIRED it’s exploring some “kinetic things.”

items.headline: Here’s Why Anthropic Is Pushing States to Regulate AI Faster
items.summary: The company endorsed landmark AI transparency laws in California and New York last year, but its head of US state and local policy says they may already be outdated.

items.headline: Please Stop Making Me Opt Out of AI
items.summary: I’m sick of “opt-out” toggles for automatically enabled generative AI features. It’s past time to make “opt in” the default setting for sensitive features.
